does: This paper announces a $10 million grant program designed to fund technical research addressing the safety and alignment of superhuman AI systems. The initiative targets four main research directions: weak-to-strong generalization (enabling weaker AI systems to learn from and align with stronger systems), interpretability (understanding how AI models make decisions), scalable oversight (developing methods to monitor and control AI systems as they become more capable), and related safety approaches. The program seeks to accelerate progress on concrete technical problems that arise when advanced AI systems surpass human capabilities, focusing on practical solutions rather than purely theoretical frameworks.
connects: This work builds on decades of AI safety research examining how to ensure advanced systems behave according to human values, extending earlier efforts by organizations like MIRI and FHI that explored alignment in principle. It responds to growing concerns about scaling from current large language models toward superhuman capabilities, building on recent empirical work in interpretability (circuits, mechanistic interpretability) and oversight techniques (RLHF, constitutional AI). The grant program acknowledges that weak-to-strong generalization and scalable oversight represent relatively new technical frontiers compared to traditional alignment theory, connecting to emerging research on how humans can maintain control over increasingly capable systems.
enables: This funding catalyzes development of concrete technical solutions for controlling superhuman AI, potentially enabling safer deployment of advanced systems at scale. Success in weak-to-strong generalization could establish training paradigms where human feedback remains effective even as AI systems exceed human capabilities in specific domains. Advances in interpretability might enable real-time monitoring and prediction of AI system failures. The research supported could establish whether current technical approaches suffice for superhuman alignment or reveal fundamental barriers requiring new breakthroughs, informing both safety research priorities and policy decisions about AI development timelines.
